---
title: "Exploratory Analysis"
format: html
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Exploratory Data Analysis

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from IPython.display import display

# Start Spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("EDAJobMarket") \
    .getOrCreate()

spark.catalog.clearCache()

# Load cleaned data created from data_cleaning.qmd
df_clean = spark.read.parquet("data/cleaned/lightcast_cleaned.parquet")
df_clean.createOrReplaceTempView("raw_jobs_clean")

# Show basic cleaned dataset information
print("Cleaned rows:", df_clean.count())
print("Cleaned columns:", len(df_clean.columns))

display(df_clean.limit(10).toPandas())

# Basic EDA summary from cleaned data
summary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_job_postings,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ID) AS unique_job_ids,
    COUNT(DISTINCT COMPANY_NAME) AS unique_companies,
    COUNT(DISTINCT STATE_NAME) AS unique_states,
    COUNT(DISTINCT NAICS_2022_6_NAME) AS unique_industries,
    COUNT(DISTINCT LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME) AS unique_occupations
FROM raw_jobs_clean
""")

display(summary.toPandas())
```

## EDA Summary Tables

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

import plotly.express as px

# Salary availability
salary_availability = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN salary IS NOT NULL AND salary > 0 THEN 'Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Salary Missing'
    END AS salary_status,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN salary IS NOT NULL AND salary > 0 THEN 'Salary Available'
        ELSE 'Salary Missing'
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""")

# Remote type distribution
remote_summary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN remote_type_name IS NULL OR remote_type_name = '[None]' THEN 'Unknown'
        ELSE remote_type_name
    END AS remote_type,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN remote_type_name IS NULL OR remote_type_name = '[None]' THEN 'Unknown'
        ELSE remote_type_name
    END
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""")

# Top states by job postings
top_states = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    state_name,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE state_name IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY state_name
ORDER BY job_count DESC
LIMIT 10
""")

# Top industries by job postings
top_industries = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    naics_2022_6_name AS industry,
    COUNT(*) AS job_count
FROM raw_jobs_clean
WHERE naics_2022_6_name IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY naics_2022_6_name
ORDER BY job_count DESC
LIMIT 10
""")

# Convert Spark results to pandas
salary_availability_pd = salary_availability.toPandas()
remote_summary_pd = remote_summary.toPandas()
top_states_pd = top_states.toPandas()
top_industries_pd = top_industries.toPandas()

# ---------- Chart 1: Salary availability ----------

fig_salary = px.bar(
    salary_availability_pd,
    x="salary_status",
    y="job_count",
    text="job_count",
    color="salary_status",
    color_discrete_map={
        "Salary Missing": "#EF553B",
        "Salary Available": "#00CC96"
    },
    title="Salary Availability in Job Postings",
    labels={
        "salary_status": "",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    }
)

fig_salary.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside",
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_salary.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=380,
    margin=dict(l=50, r=40, t=70, b=50),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    yaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)")
)

fig_salary.show()

# ---------- Chart 2: Remote type distribution ----------

fig_remote = px.bar(
    remote_summary_pd,
    x="remote_type",
    y="job_count",
    text="job_count",
    color="remote_type",
    color_discrete_map={
        "Unknown": "#636EFA",
        "Remote": "#00CC96",
        "Hybrid Remote": "#AB63FA",
        "Not Remote": "#FFA15A"
    },
    title="Remote Type Distribution",
    labels={
        "remote_type": "",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    }
)

fig_remote.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside",
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_remote.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=380,
    margin=dict(l=50, r=40, t=70, b=50),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    yaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)")
)

fig_remote.show()

# ---------- Chart 3: Top 10 states by job postings ----------

top_states_plot = top_states_pd.sort_values("job_count", ascending=True)

fig_states = px.bar(
    top_states_plot,
    x="job_count",
    y="state_name",
    text="job_count",
    color="state_name",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top 10 States by Job Postings",
    labels={
        "state_name": "",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    },
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set3
)

fig_states.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside",
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_states.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=480,
    margin=dict(l=100, r=60, t=70, b=50),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    xaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)")
)

fig_states.show()

# ---------- Chart 4: Top 10 industries by job postings ----------

top_industries_plot = top_industries_pd.sort_values("job_count", ascending=True)

fig_industries = px.bar(
    top_industries_plot,
    x="job_count",
    y="industry",
    text="job_count",
    color="industry",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top 10 Industries by Job Postings",
    labels={
        "industry": "",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    },
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Pastel
)

fig_industries.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside",
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_industries.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=560,
    margin=dict(l=260, r=60, t=70, b=50),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    xaxis=dict(gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)")
)

fig_industries.show()
```
### EDA Summary Findings
The exploratory analysis shows that the cleaned dataset contains a large number of job postings, but salary availability is limited. Out of 72,476 cleaned postings, 41,668 postings are missing salary information, while 30,808 postings include valid salary values. This means salary-related analysis should rely only on the valid salary subset, while non-salary analysis can still use the full dataset. The remote-work distribution also shows that most postings have an unknown remote classification, so remote-work comparisons should be interpreted carefully. The geographic distribution shows that Texas and California have the highest job posting counts, suggesting strong job-market activity in large and economically diverse states. The industry distribution is led by Unclassified Industry, followed by computer programming, management consulting, employment placement, and computer systems design services. Because the unclassified industry category is large, final industry-specific analysis should either treat it as a separate data-quality category or filter it out when comparing clearly defined industries.
